works.title: Girl with Balloon
works.description: One of Banksy's most iconic images, this signed AP edition represents a seminal moment in the street art market.

works.title: COMPANION (Passing Through)
works.description: A landmark piece in KAWS's sculptural practice, the Passing Through Companion has become one of the defining objects of urban contemporary collecting.

works.provenance: Estate of the artist; Private collection
works.medium: Acrylic and oil paintstick on canvas
works.title: Untitled (Head)
works.description: A rare original from Basquiat's most celebrated period, with full provenance documentation.

works.medium: Archival inkjet on Hahnemühle Photo Rag 308gsm
works.title: Gentleman with Red Dot
works.description: Part of the Miaz Brothers' celebrated "Antimatter" series, this edition captures the Italian duo's signature approach; a deliberately out-of-focus portrait that challenges the viewer's perception, dissolving identity into atmosphere. Roberto and Renato Miaz, both classically trained, spent a decade refining this technique before bringing it to the international art market.
works.edition: From the main edition of 100, signed in pencil, hand-torn edges. Released together and frequently displayed alongside "Lady with Green Background".

works.medium: Archival inkjet on Hahnemühle Photo Rag 308gsm
works.title: Lady with Green Background
works.description: A companion piece to Gentleman with Red Dot, this print exemplifies the Miaz Brothers' "Antimatter" series; a ghostly, out-of-focus female portrait in which the subject dissolves into her vivid green ground, activating the viewer's own perception to complete the image.
works.edition: Edition of 100, signed in pencil, hand-torn edges.

artists.name: Banksy
artists.bio: Banksy is an anonymous England-based street artist, political activist, and film director whose satirical street art and subversive epigrams combine dark humour with graffiti executed in a distinctive stencilling technique.

artists.name: KAWS
artists.bio: KAWS, born Brian Donnelly, is an American artist and designer who has created his own distinct visual language. Beginning with street art in the 1990s, his practice now spans painting, sculpture, and large-scale installations.

artists.name: Jean-Michel Basquiat
artists.bio: Jean-Michel Basquiat rose to fame in 1980s New York, moving from graffiti to gallery with extraordinary speed. His work remains among the most sought-after in the blue-chip contemporary market.

Roberto and Renato Miaz (b. 1965 & 1968, Monza, Italy) are an Italian sibling duo who have redefined contemporary portraiture through their "Antimatter" series — large-format works rendered in aerosol paint, in which subjects are presented deliberately out of focus, bypassing immediate reaction and inviting viewers to draw on their own memory and perception to complete the image. Classically trained and guided by the model of Renaissance artists, they first exhibited at the Viafarini Gallery in Milan in 1996, before being awarded the Arte Laguna International Art Prize in Venice in 2011 and launching the Antimatter series in 2012. They have collaborated with major brands including Adidas, Nike, Diesel, and Kenzo, and designed the cover of the New York Times Magazine in 2017.
artists.name: The Miaz Brothers
Yoon Hyup
artists.bio: Born in Seoul, South Korea in 1982, Yoon Hyup lives and works in Brooklyn, New York. Drawn to hip-hop and skate culture from an early age, he began creating large-scale murals and live paintings in the early 2000s, experimenting with lines and dots as a way of reflecting the rhythm and flow of music. Working with acrylic on canvas, he translates cityscapes, rain, and light into compositions of flat orbs and rhythmic marks, comparing his process to jazz improvisation, where each colour represents a different note and together they build a chord. Hyup has exhibited in New York, London, Seoul, Milan, Tokyo, Miami, and Hong Kong, and has collaborated with Dior, Tiffany & Co., Nike, and Maison Kitsuné, among others

artists.name: Paul Kremer
artists.bio: Paul Kremer (b. 1971, Chicago) is a contemporary American artist known for his reinterpretation of colour field painting, using overlapping hard-edged shapes to produce optical interactions rooted as much in digital software as in the work of Kenneth Noland and Jules Olitski. Self-taught as a painter, his uninflected surfaces and smooth contours have led critics to identify him as an heir to Ellsworth Kelly, with his compositions evoking both monumental architecture and broken-off glacier sections. For twenty years Kremer ran a graphic design studio, working with clients including Lou Reed, Tom Waits, and National Geographic, and he is also the inventor of the cult social media phenomenon "Great Art in Ugly Rooms," which places photo-realistic renderings of masterworks in laundromats, discount stores, and public restrooms. He lives and works in Houston, Texas.

journal.title: Understanding the edition market in 2024
journal.author: UNVLD. Editorial
journal.excerpt: The edition market has undergone a significant recalibration. Here's what serious collectors need to know.
journal.body:
The edition market has always operated on its own logic — distinct from the primary market for originals, and often misunderstood by collectors approaching it for the first time.

In 2024, that logic has shifted. Supply has expanded significantly, buyer behaviour has changed, and the gap between works that hold value and those that do not has widened considerably.

What's changed

The past three years have seen a significant increase in edition releases across all categories. Artists who previously released once or twice a year are now releasing quarterly.

What it means for collectors

Selectivity matters more than ever. The works that will hold their ground are those with genuine cultural weight — artists with sustained institutional presence, editions with low print runs and strong secondary market histories.
